title: 80,000 Australian kids aged five and under not up to date with vaccinations
content:
Find any issues using dark mode? Please let us know
Naomi Steenson doesn't want to see other parents go through what she did with her son Blake. ( ABC News: Steve Johns )
New immunisation data shows childhood vaccination coverage declined for a fifth year in 2025.
More than 80,000 Australian children aged five and under were not fully up to date with their vaccinations.
Experts warn the decline in coverage will see more severe illness and death from preventable diseases.
Naomi Steenson should be celebrating her son Blake's 29th birthday this year.
But she says it feels like little time has passed since she last held baby Blake in her arms.
"I can relive it like yesterday," she said.
As a newborn in 1997, Blake contracted whooping cough and died after a six-week battle in intensive care.
"You go from a very healthy child to virtually a baby that's blue and just gasping for breath. There was just no way of coming back," she said.
Do you have a story to share? Email Specialist.Team@abc.net.au
Blake was too young to be immunised, and a vaccine was not yet available for pregnant women in Australia to protect their newborns.
Ms Steenson said no parent should have to endure the heartache of losing a baby.
Vaccine experts are urgently sharing the same message as new data shared exclusively with the ABC shows more than 80,000 children aged five and under were not up to date with their immunisations by the end of last year.
New data shows tens of thousands of children aged five and under aren't fully immunised. ( ABC News: Billy Cooper )
The National Centre for Immunisation Research and Surveillance (NCIRS) has analysed 2025 vaccination coverage and found the proportion of one-year-olds up to date with vaccinations was at its lowest point in 12 years.
Overall, childhood vaccination coverage at the one, two and five-year milestones declined for the fifth year in a row.
"Across the board, every age group, we're continuing to see that vaccine coverage is falling and it's been falling year-on-year now since 2020," said NCIRS director Kristine Macartney.
Professor Macartney said the post-COVID-19 data for vaccination rates of two-year-olds was particularly alarming.
"We're seeing for the first time now that coverage has dropped below 90 per cent. One out of every 10 two-year-olds isn't up to date with these life-saving vaccines," she said.
summary: Find any issues using dark mode? Please let us know
author: national health equity reporters Caitlyn Gribbin and Rachel Carbonell
